Title: Bamana Ci Wara Headdress
Description: Among the Bamana, oral traditions credit the mythical being Ci Wara (half mortal and half animal) with introducing agriculture to an agricultural society that still relies heavily on subsistence farming. Carved ci wara headdresses honor this being, who taught humans to cultivate the land before withdrawing into the earth when they became wasteful; his spirit was then enshrined in a boli and represented through these sculptures. The headdresses combine antelope features with those of animals like the aardvark and pangolin, whose natural behaviors mirror essential farming tasks, and they feature tapered antelope-inspired forms, aardvark references, and intricate openwork and geometric designs. Performed in male–female pairs by skilled dancers wearing long raffia costumes, ci wara masquerades praise and encourage farmers, symbolizing the elemental forces of sun, earth, water, and humanity, with women contributing through songs of praise. This particular work was acquired by Lawrence Arthur Jones, the first Chair of the Department of Art at Jackson State University.
Subject: agriculture; mythology; fertility; performance; subsistence farming

Title: Untitled object 2
Description: This standing power figure is attributed to the Kongo cultural region and reflects the tradition of nkisi figures created to mediate between the human and spiritual realms. Such figures were activated through ritual practices conducted by ritual specialists and were believed to house spiritual forces capable of protection, healing, oath enforcement, and conflict resolution. The prominent abdominal bundle likely once contained sacred substances that endowed the figure with efficacy. The composed facial expression, upright posture, and material additions emphasize restraint, vigilance, and latent spiritual authority rather than overt aggression.
Subject: spiritual power; protection; healing; ancestral mediation; ritual practice

Title: Tabwa Mask
Description: This mask is attributed to the Tabwa people and reflects Central African traditions in which masks function as instruments of moral authority and ancestral presence. Characterized by geometric facial segmentation, narrow eyes, and a restrained expression, the mask emphasizes composure, wisdom, and social discipline rather than individual portraiture. Such masks were typically used in ceremonial contexts connected to initiation, funerary rites, or social regulation, where performance activated their meaning. The incised surface and dark patina suggest sustained ritual use and reinforce the mask’s role as a mediator between the living community and the ancestral realm.
Subject: ancestral authority; moral instruction; initiation; funerary practices; performance

Title: Tabwa Buffalo Slit Drum
Description: This wooden slit drum is carved in the form of a buffalo, an animal associated with strength, endurance, and social authority in Central African cultures. Among the Tabwa, such drums were used for communication and ritual purposes, including signaling, ceremonial performances, and community gatherings. The elongated body functions as a resonating chamber, while the carved zigzag motifs and stylized animal features emphasize both visual symbolism and acoustic function. The drum reflects the integration of sound, sculpture, and social meaning in Tabwa artistic traditions.
Subject: communication; ritual sound; buffalo symbolism; community gatherings; social authority

Title: Bembe Ibulu Iya (Janus Mask)
Description: This mask is attributed to the Bembe people and reflects Central African masking traditions associated with initiation, healing, and social transition. Characterized by a rounded form, large circular eye surrounds, and red and white painted facial zones, the mask emphasizes visual contrast and presence rather than aggressive abstraction. Raffia elements formed part of the masquerade costume, activating the mask through movement and performance within community rituals.

Title: Untitled object 8
Description: This object is a lukasa, a memory board used by the Luba people as a mnemonic device for preserving and transmitting historical knowledge. Lukasa were employed by members of the Mbudye society to recall genealogies, political authority, moral teachings, and significant events. The incised surface patterns and sculpted seated figure functioned as tactile and visual prompts activated through recitation and instruction rather than visual reading alone.
Subject: historical memory; political authority; oral tradition; mnemonic device; governance

Title: Untitled object 9
Description: This standing female figure is attributed to the Baule people and represents a blolo bla, or female spirit spouse. Title: Untitled object 11
Description: This banda (also known as kumbaruba) headdress represents a powerful composite being combining human and animal forms. The elongated horizontal structure incorporates a human face with Baga scarification and an elaborately braided female coiffure, joined to the jaw of a crocodile with visible teeth, along with antelope horns, a serpent’s body, and a chameleon’s tail. Historically a sacred figure associated with protection and male initiation rites, banda was accessible only to senior elders. Today it is performed in masquerades accompanied by drumming and dynamic choreography that evokes animal movement.
Subject: masquerade; animal symbolism; initiation; protection; performance

Title: Untitled object 112
Description: A small seated Janus-style power figure carved with two opposed heads sharing a single body, featuring compact proportions, bent knees, hands placed along the torso, and simplified facial features, a dual-aspect form associated with vigilance, balance, and the ability to see in multiple directions, consistent with western Central Congolese sculptural traditions
Subject: protective power; divination; ritual mediation; embodiment of duality and vigilance
